answer:
**Bang-e-Dara Part 3, Chapter 26** by Allama Iqbal is an untitled poem (indicated by a line of decorative equals signs as its chapter title), presented here in English translation. It evokes a mystical night scene under moonlight, addressing a restless wanderer with metaphors of nature, stars, and the poet's unquiet heart.[^1]

> Why in my moonlight do you wander, restless and astray?  
> Silent as a flower, like a fragrance scattered on its way.[^2]
> 
> Perhaps you are the jeweler of the pearls that are the night,  
> Or a fish that swims within my flowing river of the light.[^3]
> 
> Or are you a fallen star, from my own brow cast down,  
> Who left the heights of glory to reside within this town?[^4]
> 
> The very lute of being has its music put to rest,  
> My mirror holds the sleeping dream of life within its breast.[^5]
> 
> The whirlpool's eye has closed within the river's deepest part,  
> The restless wave against the shore has stilled its beating heart.[^6]
> 
> This earthly town, once so alive and full of clamorous strife,  
> Now sleeps as if it never held a single breathing life.[^7]
> 
> And yet the Poet's heart, a stranger still to peace,  
> How have you stayed so free of my spell's release?[^8]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 26 [chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-89c390](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-89c390).
[^3]: Ibid., [p-576eeb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-576eeb).
[^4]: Ibid., [p-d681a6](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-d681a6).
[^5]: Ibid., [p-6e43ff](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-6e43ff).
[^6]: Ibid., [p-1772fb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-1772fb).
[^7]: Ibid., [p-24207e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-24207e).
[^8]: Ibid., [p-ae494e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/26/translation/#p-ae494e).
